instruction: Analyze the following network configuration and describe its functionality in natural language:
input: router bgp 45000
output: Configure Router B for BGP in AS 45000 with router-id 172.17.1.99. Suppress automatic IPv4 unicast activation, log neighbor events, and set BGP timers to keepalive 70 and hold 120. Form external BGP sessions to 192.168.1.2 (AS 40000) and 192.168.3.2 (AS 50000), tagging the latter as "finance." Under the IPv4 unicast address family, activate both neighbors, disable auto-summary and synchronization, and originate route 172.17.1.0/24. Under the IPv4 multicast address family, activate only 192.168.3.2 with a 25-second update interval, again disabling auto-summary and synchronization, and originate the same 172.17.1.0/24 network.

instruction: Generate the network configuration for the following requirement:
input:
With the following configuration, OSPF external routes with tags 1, 2, 3, and 5 are redistributed into RIP with metrics of
                                 1, 1, 5, and 5, respectively. The OSPF routes with a tag of 4 are not redistributed.
output:
router rip
 redistribute ospf 109 route-map 1
!
route-map 1 permit
 match tag 1 2
 set metric 1
!
route-map 1 permit 
 match tag 3
 set metric 5
!
route-map 1 deny
 match tag 4
!
route map 1 permit
 match tag 5
 set metric 5

instruction: Analyze the following network configuration and describe its functionality in natural language:
input:
router isis
 redistribute ospf 109 route-map 2
 redistribute iso-igrp nsfnet route-map 3
!
route-map 2 permit
 match route-type external
 match tag 5
 set metric 5
 set level level-2
!
route-map 3 permit
 match address 2000
 set metric 30
output:
The following example redistributes two types of routes into the integrated IS-IS routing table (supporting both IP and CLNS).
                                 The first type is OSPF external IP routes with tag 5; these routes are inserted into Level 2 IS-IS link state packets (LSPs)
                                 with a metric of 5. The second type is ISO-IGRP derived CLNS prefix routes that match CLNS access list 2000; these routes
                                 will be redistributed into IS-IS as Level 2 LSPs with a metric of 30.

instruction: Generate the network configuration for the following requirement:
input:
The following example illustrates the assignment of four area IDs to four IP address ranges. In the example, OSPF routing
                                    process 109 is initialized, and four OSPF areas are defined: 10.9.50.0, 2, 3, and 0. Areas 10.9.50.0, 2, and 3 mask specific
                                    address ranges, and area 0 enables OSPF forallothernetworks.
output:
router ospf 109
 network 192.168.10.0 0.0.0.255 area 10.9.50.0
 network 192.168.20.0 0.0.255.255 area 2
 network 192.168.30.0 0.0.0.255 area 3
 network 192.168.40.0 255.255.255.255 area 0
!
! Interface Ethernet0 is in area 10.9.50.0:
interface ethernet 0
 ip address 192.168.10.5 255.255.255.0
!
! Interface Ethernet1 is in area 2:
interface ethernet 1
 ip address 192.168.20.5 255.255.255.0
!
! Interface Ethernet2 is in area 2:
interface ethernet 2
 ip address 192.168.20.7 255.255.255.0
!
! Interface Ethernet3 is in area 3:
interface ethernet 3
 ip address 192.169.30.5 255.255.255.0
!
! Interface Ethernet4 is in area 0:
interface ethernet 4
 ip address 192.168.40.1 255.255.255.0
!
! Interface Ethernet5 is in area 0:
interface ethernet 5
 ip address 192.168.40.12 255.255.0.0

instruction: Generate the network configuration for the following requirement:
input:
The following example shows how to use an address family to configure a peer group so that all members of the peer group
                                 are both unicast- and multicast-capable:
output:
router bgp 45000
 neighbor 192.168.1.2 remote-as 40000
 neighbor 192.168.3.2 remote-as 50000
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  neighbor mygroup peer-group
  neighbor 192.168.1.2 peer-group mygroup
  neighbor 192.168.3.2 peer-group mygroup
router bgp 45000
 neighbor 192.168.1.2 remote-as 40000
 neighbor 192.168.3.2 remote-as 50000
 address-family ipv4 multicast
  neighbor mygroup peer-group
  neighbor 192.168.1.2 peer-group mygroup
  neighbor 192.168.3.2 peer-group mygroup
  neighbor 192.168.1.2 activate
  neighbor 192.168.3.2 activate

instruction: Analyze the following network configuration and describe its functionality in natural language:
input:
router rip
 redistribute ospf 109 route-map 1
!

 match tag 1 2
 set metric 1
!
route-map 1 permit 
 match tag 3
 set metric 5
!
route-map 1 deny
 match tag 4
!
route map 1 permit
 match tag 5
 set metric 5
output:

                                 1, 1, 5, and 5, respectively. The OSPF routes with a tag of 4 are not redistributed.

instruction: Analyze the following network configuration and describe its functionality in natural language:
input:
router ospf 109
 network 192.168.10.0 0.0.0.255 area 10.9.50.0
 network 192.168.20.0 0.0.255.255 area 2
 network 192.168.30.0 0.0.0.255 area 3
 network 192.168.40.0 255.255.255.255 area 0
!
! Interface Ethernet0 is in area 10.9.50.0:
interface ethernet 0
 ip address 192.168.10.5 255.255.255.0
!
! Interface Ethernet1 is in area 2:
interface ethernet 1
 ip address 192.168.20.5 255.255.255.0
!
! Interface Ethernet2 is in area 2:
interface ethernet 2
 ip address 192.168.20.7 255.255.255.0
!
! Interface Ethernet3 is in area 3:
interface ethernet 3
 ip address 192.169.30.5 255.255.255.0
!
! Interface Ethernet4 is in area 0:
interface ethernet 4
 ip address 192.168.40.1 255.255.255.0
!
! Interface Ethernet5 is in area 0:
interface ethernet 5
 ip address 192.168.40.12 255.255.0.0
output:
The following example illustrates the assignment of four area IDs to four IP address ranges. In the example, OSPF routing
                                    process 109 is initialized, and four OSPF areas are defined: 10.9.50.0, 2, 3, and 0. Areas 10.9.50.0, 2, and 3 mask specific
                                    address ranges, and area 0 enables OSPF forallothernetworks.

instruction: Analyze the following network configuration and describe its functionality in natural language:
input:
router isis
 redistribute ospf 109 route-map 2
 redistribute iso-igrp nsfnet route-map 3
!
route-map 2 permit
 match route-type external
 match tag 5
 set metric 5
 set level level-2
!
route-map 3 permit
 match address 2000
 set metric 30
output: "The goal here is a dual redistribution into IS-IS Level 2: for IP, redistribute OSPF 109's external routes that carry tag 5, setting their IS-IS metric to 5. For CLNS, redistribute ISO-IGRP nsfnet routes that match access-list 2000, with a metric of 30. This populates the IS-IS LSPs with both OSPF external IP routes and CLNS prefix routes from ISO-IGRP."

instruction: Generate the network configuration for the following requirement:
input: "I need to set up IS-IS to pull in two kinds of external routes. First, grab any OSPF external routes from process 109 that have a tag of 5, and inject them into the Level 2 IS-IS database with a metric of 5. Second, take any CLNS prefix routes learned via ISO-IGRP from the nsfnet process that match access-list 2000, and redistribute those into Level 2 IS-IS LSPs with a metric of 30."
output:
router isis
 redistribute ospf 109 route-map 2
 redistribute iso-igrp nsfnet route-map 3
!
route-map 2 permit
 match route-type external
 match tag 5
 set metric 5
 set level level-2
!
route-map 3 permit
 match address 2000
 set metric 30

instruction: Generate the network configuration for the following requirement:
input:
The following example redistributes two types of routes into the integrated IS-IS routing table (supporting both IP and CLNS).
                                 The first type is OSPF external IP routes with tag 5; these routes are inserted into Level 2 IS-IS link state packets (LSPs)
                                 with a metric of 5. The second type is ISO-IGRP derived CLNS prefix routes that match CLNS access list 2000; these routes
                                 will be redistributed into IS-IS as Level 2 LSPs with a metric of 30.
output:
router isis
 redistribute ospf 109 route-map 2
 redistribute iso-igrp nsfnet route-map 3
!
route-map 2 permit
 match route-type external
 match tag 5
 set metric 5
 set level level-2
!
route-map 3 permit
 match address 2000
 set metric 30

instruction: Generate the network configuration for the following requirement:
input: "The goal here is a dual redistribution into IS-IS Level 2: for IP, redistribute OSPF 109's external routes that carry tag 5, setting their IS-IS metric to 5. For CLNS, redistribute ISO-IGRP nsfnet routes that match access-list 2000, with a metric of 30. This populates the IS-IS LSPs with both OSPF external IP routes and CLNS prefix routes from ISO-IGRP."
output:
router isis
 redistribute ospf 109 route-map 2
 redistribute iso-igrp nsfnet route-map 3
!
route-map 2 permit
 match route-type external
 match tag 5
 set metric 5
 set level level-2
!
route-map 3 permit
 match address 2000
 set metric 30

instruction: Analyze the following network configuration and describe its functionality in natural language:
input:
router isis

 redistribute iso-igrp nsfnet route-map 3
!

 match route-type external
 match tag 5
 set metric 5
 set level level-2
!
route-map 3 permit
 match address 2000
 set metric 30
output: "I need to set up IS-IS to pull in two kinds of external routes. First, grab any OSPF external routes from process 109 that have a tag of 5, and inject them into the Level 2 IS-IS database with a metric of 5. Second, take any CLNS prefix routes learned via ISO-IGRP from the nsfnet process that match access-list 2000, and redistribute those into Level 2 IS-IS LSPs with a metric of 30."
